forecasts.reasoning: John Morgan has teased a gubernatorial run and even floated creating a new political party. While Florida law requires him to register for a new party 365 days before the June 2026 qualifying deadline (meaning by June 12, 2025), two factors strongly weigh against an announcement of candidacy before July 1, 2025. First, Morgan has a long track record of floating political runs without following through. Second, he explicitly stated in mid-May 2025 that he would prefer a 'three-month sprint' over an '18-month marathon', indicating he wants to delay any official campaign as long as possible. Even if he establishes a new party in June 2025, he does not need to formally announce his own candidacy at that time. With less than a month remaining before the July 1 deadline and no recent steps taken, the probability of him formally declaring is low.

The Bulgargaz-Botas agreement has no termination clause, and Bulgarian officials (including the Energy Minister as recently as late May 2025) have confirmed that unilaterally breaking it would cost Bulgaria an estimated BGN 4 billion ($2.2 billion) in penalties. While Bulgargaz has ceased payments and politicians like Boyko Borissov have appealed directly to President Erdogan to cancel the deal, Turkey has strong leverage and seems to be focusing on renegotiation/amendments rather than outright cancellation. The process is progressing slowly, and an official announcement of cancellation by either side before July 1, 2025 (within the next 25 days) is highly unlikely given the massive financial liabilities and the preference for restructuring the contract instead.

Sudan has been engulfed in a catastrophic civil war since April 2023. As of late January 2025, Sudan remained one of the 7 African Union signatories that had not yet ratified the AfCFTA agreement. Furthermore, because the Sudanese government lacks a functioning, unified legislative body capable of domestically codifying and promulgating international trade treaties, they face insurmountable structural barriers to doing so. With only 25 days remaining until the July 1, 2025 deadline, it is virtually impossible for Sudan to complete the ratification process. As such, the forecast is set at the minimum allowable boundary of 2%.

John Morgan has repeatedly flirted with the idea of a 2026 run for Florida Governor, and recently discussed launching a new third party. However, at a May 14, 2025 Capital Tiger Bay luncheon, he explicitly stated that he intends to wait until September 2026 (after the major party primaries) before deciding whether to enter the race. While Florida law requires him to formally register with his new third party by June 12, 2025, to run on its ticket (365 days before the June 12, 2026, qualifying deadline), changing his voter affiliation or forming a party does not constitute formally announcing his own candidacy. Since he clearly and recently stated his timeline is over a year from now, an announcement before July 1, 2025 is highly improbable unless he makes an abrupt structural change to his strategy or accidentally declares while filing party paperwork.

The Bulgargaz-BOTAŞ agreement, signed for 13 years in 2023, is severely detrimental to Bulgaria, costing roughly €500k per day for unused capacity. This has led to massive financial losses, putting Bulgargaz on the verge of bankruptcy. In May 2024, the Bulgarian parliament legally instructed the Energy Minister to renegotiate the deal, and political leader Boyko Borissov directly urged Turkish President Erdogan to allow for a mutual cancellation. Furthermore, the contract is under EU Commission scrutiny for potentially violating European competition laws.

However, terminating the agreement is highly complex. The contract reportedly lacks a standard termination clause. Unilateral termination by Bulgaria could trigger an ICC arbitration claim from Turkey for the entirely of the contract's anticipated revenue (~€1.4 to €2.1 billion). Because the penalty for canceling is almost as high as the cost of keeping it, Bulgaria faces a massive barrier to simply walking away, making a drawn-out renegotiation or prolonged default more likely than an explicit, immediate, and formal "cancellation." 

Yet, there are legitimate pathways to a formal cancellation before July 1, 2025 (a ~13-month horizon from the latest May 2024 events). The EU Commission could rule the contract invalid under EU law, providing Bulgaria a legal mandate (and shield) to formally terminate it. Alternatively, Bulgaria might unilaterally repudiate the contract by claiming it was signed corruptly by an unauthorized caretaker government, forcing it into arbitration. The resolution criteria clearly specify that unilateral termination, even if disputed by BOTAŞ, resolves the question as YES. I place the probability at 38%, balancing the intense domestic/EU political will to cancel the agreement against the massive financial and diplomatic deterrents to formal termination.

The African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) has been ratified by 47 or 48 of the 54 signatory countries, but Sudan is consistently listed among the seven countries that have not yet ratified it (alongside Benin, Liberia, Libya, Madagascar, Somalia, and South Sudan), with sources confirming this status up to at least January 2025. Sudan has been consumed by a devastating, nationwide civil war since April 2023, leaving it without a functioning standardized legislative body capable of prioritizing and passing an international trade ratification. With the forecast date of June 6, 2025, there are only 25 days remaining before the July 1, 2025 deadline. There has been no recent reporting indicating that the Sovereign Council intends to ratify AfCFTA by decree in the coming weeks. Given the ongoing existential conflict, economic collapse, and complete lack of recent progress on this front, the probability of Sudan ratifying the agreement before July 1, 2025 is extremely close to zero.
